rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical reductive Giese-type coupling of unactivated alkyl chlorides with electron-deficient olefins (acrylates) catalyzed by cobalt salen complexes
electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of Co(II/III) salen to a Co(I) nucleophile that activates the alkyl chloride via inner-sphere oxidative addition to form Co(III)-alkyl; a second cathodic reduction of the Giese adduct (alpha-acyl radical) to the enolate is required for product formation. Zn sacrificial anode supplies counter-oxidation.

key_intermediates.name: enolate (from second reduction of Giese adduct)
acrylate oligomers (polymerization side products without reductant)

electron_flow: Cathode reduces Co(II) salen to Co(I), which displaces chloride from R-Cl to form a Co(III)-alkyl resting state; thermal Co(III)-C homolysis releases an alkyl radical that adds to acrylate (Giese addition); the resulting alpha-ester radical is reduced (cathodically) to an enolate, which is protonated by nBuOH/H2O to deliver the product.
rate_determining_step: Co(III)-C homolysis releasing the alkyl radical (modulated by salen steric bulk; faster with bulky Co(L3) for primary substrates)

probe_reagents.name: 6-chlorohex-1-ene (radical clock)
probe_reagents.observation: Cyclized:uncyclized product ratio 3.6:1 at 5 mol% Co(Me4Salen); ratio decreases with higher catalyst loading (2.8 at 1.1% to 1.9 at 20%), consistent with free alkyl radical intermediate whose lifetime depends on Co concentration

probe_reagents.name: D2O (vs H2O)
probe_reagents.observation: ~95% single deuterium incorporation at alpha-position of ester product, confirming protonation of an enolate intermediate (i.e., second reduction of Giese adduct radical)

probe_reagents.name: Co(III)(L2)-nOctyl stoichiometric
probe_reagents.observation: Heating in presence of butyl acrylate without reductant gives Giese product plus oligomers (radical polymerization); under electroreductive conditions, oligomers suppressed and product yield increases, supporting cathodic reduction of Giese adduct to enolate

probe_reagents.name: Co(L1) added to Co(L3) reactions (inhibition study)
probe_reagents.observation: Increasing loading of less bulky Co(L1) decreases yield with primary electrophiles, implying formation of stable Co(III)-R adduct that retards homolysis

probe_reagents.name: Constant-potential electrolysis at -1.7, -1.9, -2.2 V (CV referenced)
probe_reagents.observation: Product formation and RCl consumption rates independent of applied potential; near-quantitative yield even at mild -1.7 V (E1/2 of catalyst), indicating direct reduction of Co(III)-alkyl is not required—radical generation proceeds via thermal homolysis

notes: Steric tuning of salen ligand (Co(L3) = Me4Salen bulky vs Co(L1) = CySalen less bulky) controls Co(III)-C homolysis rate: bulky ligand accelerates homolysis (preferred for primary alkyl chlorides), less bulky for secondary/tertiary. An alternative mechanism in which the Co(III)-alkyl is reduced directly at the cathode to release the radical is ruled out by potential-independence experiments — thermal homolysis is operative. Without cathodic reduction of the Giese adduct radical, acrylate polymerization dominates, indicating that the second cathodic electron (to form the enolate) is essential.
